text.DRB: And Moses said to the Lord: The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou did charge, and command, saying: Set limits about the mount, and sanctify it.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to him, “Go down, and come up bringing Aaron with you; but do not let either the priests or the people break through to come up to the Lord; otherwise he will break out against them.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto him—Away, down, then shalt thou come up, thou and Aaron with thee,—but as for the priests and the people, let it not be that they press through to come up unto Yahweh lest he break in upon them.
text.DRB: And the Lord said to him: Go, get thee down: and thou shalt come up, thou and Aaron with thee: but let not the priests and the people pass the limits, nor come up to the Lord, lest he kill them.

text.NRSV: So Moses went down to the people and told them.
text.Rotherham: So Moses went down unto the people,—and said [these things] unto them.
text.DRB: And Moses went down to the people and told them all.

text.NRSV: Then God spoke all these words:
text.Rotherham: And God spake all these words saying:—
text.DRB: And the Lord spoke all these words:

text.NRSV: I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery;
text.Rotherham: I, am Yahweh thy God,—who have brought thee forth out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of servants:—
text.DRB: I am the Lord thy God, who brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.

text.NRSV: you shall have no other gods before me.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not have other gods besides me.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not have strange gods before me.

text.NRSV: You shall not make for yourself an idol, whether in the form of anything that is in heaven above, or that is on the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not make to thee an image, or any form, that is in the heavens above,—or that is in the earth beneath,—or that is in the waters beneath the earth:
text.DRB: Thou shalt not make to thyself a graven thing, nor the likeness of any thing that is in heaven above, or in the earth beneath, nor of those things that are in the waters under the earth.

text.NRSV: You shall not bow down to them or worship them; for I the Lord your God am a jealous God, punishing children for the iniquity of parents, to the third and the fourth generation of those who reject me,
text.Rotherham: thou shalt not bow thyself down to them nor be led to serve them—For, I, Yahweh, thy God, am a jealous GOD, visiting the iniquity of fathers upon sons, unto three [generations] and, unto four, of them that hate me;
text.DRB: Thou shalt not adore them, nor serve them: I am the Lord thy God, mighty, jealous, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children, unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me:

text.NRSV: but showing steadfast love to the thousandth generation of those who love me and keep my commandments.
text.Rotherham: but shewing lovingkindness unto thousands [of generations],—of them who love me, and keep my commandments.
text.DRB: And shewing mercy unto thousands to them that love me, and keep my commandments.

text.NRSV: You shall not make wrongful use of the name of the Lord your God, for the Lord will not acquit anyone who misuses his name.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not utter the name of Yahweh thy God for falsehood, for Yahweh will not let him go unpunished who uttereth his name for falsehood.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not take the name of the Lord thy God in vain: for the Lord will not hold him guiltless that shall take the name of the Lord his God in vain.

text.NRSV: Remember the sabbath day, and keep it holy.
text.Rotherham: Remember the sabbath day, to hallow it:
text.DRB: Remember that thou keep holy the sabbath day.

text.NRSV: Six days you shall labor and do all your work.
text.Rotherham: Six days, shalt thou labour, and do all thy work;
text.DRB: Six days shalt thou labour, and shalt do all thy works.

text.NRSV: But the seventh day is a sabbath to the Lord your God; you shall not do any work—you, your son or your daughter, your male or female slave, your livestock, or the alien resident in your towns.
text.Rotherham: but, the seventh day, is a sabbath unto Yahweh thy God,—thou shalt do no work, thou nor thy son nor thy daughter nor thy servant, nor thy handmaid, nor thy beast, nor thy sojourner who is within thy gates,
text.DRB: But on the seventh day is the sabbath of the Lord thy God: thou shalt do no work on it, thou nor thy son, nor thy daughter, nor thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thy beast, nor the stranger that is within thy gates.

text.NRSV: For in six days the Lord made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them, but rested the seventh day; therefore the Lord blessed the sabbath day and consecrated it.
text.Rotherham: For, in six days, did Yahweh make the heavens and the earth, [and] the sea—and all that in them is, and rested on the seventh day,—for this cause, Yahweh blessed the sabbath day and hallowed it.
text.DRB: For in six days the Lord made heaven and earth, and the sea, and all things that are in them, and rested on the seventh day: therefore the Lord blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it.

text.NRSV: Honor your father and your mother, so that your days may be long in the land that the Lord your God is giving you.
text.Rotherham: Honour thy father, and thy mother,—that thy days may be prolonged upon the soil, which Yahweh thy God is about to give unto thee.
text.DRB: Honour thy father and thy mother, that thou mayest be longlived upon the land which the Lord thy God will give thee.

text.NRSV: You shall not murder.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not commit murder.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not kill.

text.NRSV: You shall not commit adultery.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not commit adultery.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not commit adultery.

text.NRSV: You shall not steal.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not steal.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not steal.

text.NRSV: You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not testify against thy neighbour, with a witness of falsehood.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour.

text.NRSV: You shall not covet your neighbor’s house; you shall not covet your neighbor’s wife, or male or female slave, or ox, or donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not covet, thy neighbour’s house,—thou shalt not covet, thy neighbour’s wife nor his servant nor his handmaid nor his ox, nor his ass, nor anything which belongeth unto thy neighbour.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's house: neither shalt thou desire his wife, nor his servant, nor his handmaid, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor any thing that is his.

text.NRSV: When all the people witnessed the thunder and lightning, the sound of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking, they were afraid and trembled and stood at a distance,
text.Rotherham: And, all the people, were witnessing the voices and the torches, and the sound of the horn, and the mountain smoking,—so then the people were struck with awe and shrank back, and stood afar off.
text.DRB: And all the people saw the voices and the flames, and the sound of the trumpet, and the mount smoking: and being terrified and struck with fear, they stood afar off,

text.NRSV: and said to Moses, “You speak to us, and we will listen; but do not let God speak to us, or we will die.”
text.Rotherham: And they said unto Moses, Speak, thou, with us, and we will hear,—but let not God speak with us lest we die.
text.DRB: Saying to Moses: Speak thou to us, and we will hear: let not the Lord speak to us, lest we die.

text.NRSV: Moses said to the people, “Do not be afraid; for God has come only to test you and to put the fear of him upon you so that you do not sin.”
text.Rotherham: And Moses said unto the people—Do not fear, for to the end he may prove you, hath God come,—and to the end the reverence of him may be upon your faces that ye sin not.
text.DRB: And Moses said to the people: Fear not: for God is come to prove you, and that the dread of him might be in you, and you should not sin.

text.NRSV: Then the people stood at a distance, while Moses drew near to the thick darkness where God was.
text.Rotherham: So then the people stood afar off, and, Moses, approached unto the thick gloom, where God was.
text.DRB: And the people stood afar off. But Moses went to the dark cloud wherein God was.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Moses: Thus you shall say to the Israelites: “You have seen for yourselves that I spoke with you from heaven.
text.Rotherham: Then said Yahweh unto Moses, Thus, shalt thou say, unto the sons of Israel,—Ye yourselves, have seen that out of the heavens, have I spoken with you.
text.DRB: And the Lord said to Moses: Thus shalt thou say to the children of Israel: You have seen that I have spoken to you from heaven.

text.NRSV: You shall make no gods of silver alongside me, nor shall you make for yourselves gods of gold.
text.Rotherham: Ye shall not make [aught] beside me,—neither gods of silver, nor gods of gold, shall ye make to yourselves.
text.DRB: You shall not make gods of silver, nor shall you make to yourselves gods of gold.

text.NRSV: You need make for me only an altar of earth and sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and your offerings of well-being, your sheep and your oxen; in every place where I cause my name to be remembered I will come to you and bless you.
text.Rotherham: An altar of earth, shalt thou make to me, then shalt thou offer thereupon thine ascending-sacrifices and thy peace-offerings, thy sheep, and thine oxen. In every place where I may mention my name, will I come in unto thee and will bless thee.
text.DRB: You shall make an altar of earth unto me, and you shall offer upon it your holocausts and peace offerings, your sheep and oxen, in every place where the memory of my name shall be: I will come to thee, and will bless thee.

text.NRSV: But if you make for me an altar of stone, do not build it of hewn stones; for if you use a chisel upon it you profane it.
text.Rotherham: But if, an altar of stones, thou wilt make to me, thou shalt not build them hewn,—for as soon as thou hast lifted, thy sharp tool, thereupon, thou hast profaned it;
text.DRB: And if thou make an altar of stone unto me, thou shalt not build it of hewn stones: for if thou lift up a tool upon it, it shall be defiled.

text.NRSV: You shall not go up by steps to my altar, so that your nakedness may not be exposed on it.”
text.Rotherham: neither shalt thou go up, by steps unto mine altar,—lest by any means thy shame be discovered thereupon.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not go up by steps unto my altar, lest thy nakedness be discovered.

text.NRSV: These are the ordinances that you shall set before them:
text.Rotherham: Now, these are the regulations which thou shalt put before them.
text.DRB: These are the judgments which thou shalt set before them.

text.NRSV: When you buy a male Hebrew slave, he shall serve six years, but in the seventh he shall go out a free person, without debt.
text.Rotherham: When thou shalt acquire a servant who is a Hebrew, six years, shall he serve,—but in the seventh shall he go out freely—for nought.
text.DRB: If thou buy a Hebrew servant, six years shall he serve thee: in the seventh he shall go out free for nothing.

text.NRSV: If he comes in single, he shall go out single; if he comes in married, then his wife shall go out with him.
text.Rotherham: If, by himself, he came in, by himself, shall he go out,—if he was married, then shall his wife go out with him.
text.DRB: With what raiment he came in, with the like let him go out: if having a wife, his wife also shall go out with him.

text.NRSV: If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the wife and her children shall be her master’s and he shall go out alone.
text.Rotherham: If his lord gave him a wife, and she have borne him sons or daughters, the wife and her children shall be her lords, and, he shall go out by himself.
text.DRB: But if his master gave him a wife, and she hath borne sons and daughters: the woman and her children shall be her master's: but he himself shall go out with his raiment.

text.NRSV: But if the slave declares, “I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out a free person,”
text.Rotherham: But, if the servant shall plainly say, I love my lord, and my wife and my sons,—I will not go out free,
text.DRB: And if the servant shall say: I love my master and my wife and children, I will not go out free:

text.NRSV: then his master shall bring him before God. He shall be brought to the door or the doorpost; and his master shall pierce his ear with an awl; and he shall serve him for life.
text.Rotherham: then shall his lord bring him near unto God, and shall bring him near unto the door, or unto the door-post,—and his lord shall pierce his ear with an awl, so shall he serve him all his life.
text.DRB: His master shall bring him to the gods , and he shall be set to the door and the posts, and he shall bore his ear through with an awl: and he shall be his servant for ever.

text.NRSV: When a man sells his daughter as a slave, she shall not go out as the male slaves do.
text.Rotherham: And when a man shall sell his daughter to be a handmaid, she shall not go out according to the out-going of the men-servants,
text.DRB: If any man sell his daughter to be a servant, she shall not go out as bondwomen are wont to go out.

text.NRSV: If she does not please her master, who designated her for himself, then he shall let her be redeemed; he shall have no right to sell her to a foreign people, since he has dealt unfairly with her.
text.Rotherham: If she is uncomely in the eyes of her lord, who hath not assigned her in marriage, then shall he suffer her to be redeemed: to a strange people, shall he not have power to sell her in that he hath dealt treacherously with her.
text.DRB: If she displease the eyes of her master to whom she was delivered, he shall let her go: but he shall have no power to sell her to a foreign nation, if he despise her.

text.NRSV: If he designates her for his son, he shall deal with her as with a daughter.
text.Rotherham: And if, to his son, he assign her, according to the custom for daughters, shall he do for her.
text.DRB: But if he have betrothed her to his son, he shall deal with her after the manner of daughters.

text.NRSV: If he takes another wife to himself, he shall not diminish the food, clothing, or marital rights of the first wife.
text.Rotherham: If he take to himself another, her food her clothing, and her marriage-right, shall he not withdraw.
text.DRB: And if he take another wife for him, he shall provide her a marriage, and raiment, neither shall he refuse the price of her chastity.

text.NRSV: And if he does not do these three things for her, she shall go out without debt, without payment of money.
text.Rotherham: But if these three, he will not do for her, then shall she go out for nought, without silver.
text.DRB: If he do not these three things, she shall go out free without money.

text.NRSV: Whoever strikes a person mortally shall be put to death.
text.Rotherham: He that smiteth a man, so that he die, shall surely be put to death.
text.DRB: He that striketh a man with a will to kill him, shall be put to death.

text.NRSV: If it was not premeditated, but came about by an act of God, then I will appoint for you a place to which the killer may flee.
text.Rotherham: But he who hath not lain in wait, but, God, hath occasioned him to come to his hand, then will I appoint for thee a place, whither he may flee.
text.DRB: But he that did not lie in wait for him, but God delivered him into his hands: I will appoint thee a place to which he must flee.

text.NRSV: But if someone willfully attacks and kills another by treachery, you are to take the killer from my altar for execution.
text.Rotherham: But, when a man shall act presumptuously against his neighbour to slay him with guile, from mine altar, shalt thou take him to die.
text.DRB: If a man kill his neighbour on set purpose and by lying in wait for him: thou shalt take him away from my altar, that he may die.

text.NRSV: Whoever strikes father or mother shall be put to death.
text.Rotherham: And, he that smiteth his father or his mother, shall, surely be put to death.
text.DRB: He that striketh his father or mother, shall be put to death.

text.NRSV: Whoever kidnaps a person, whether that person has been sold or is still held in possession, shall be put to death.
text.Rotherham: And, he that stealeth a man of the sons of Israel, and selleth him, or he be found in his hand, shall, surely be put to death.
text.DRB: He that shall steal a man, and sell him, being convicted of guilt, shall be put to death.

text.NRSV: Whoever curses father or mother shall be put to death.
text.Rotherham: And he who revileth his father or his mother, shall, surely be put to death.
text.DRB: He that curseth his father, or mother, shall die the death.

text.NRSV: When individuals quarrel and one strikes the other with a stone or fist so that the injured party, though not dead, is confined to bed,
text.Rotherham: And when men strive together, and one shall smite the other, with a stone or with his fist,—and he die not, but shall fall to his bed;—
text.DRB: If men quarrel, and the one strike his neighbour with a stone or with his fist, and he die not, but keepeth his bed:

text.NRSV: but recovers and walks around outside with the help of a staff, then the assailant shall be free of liability, except to pay for the loss of time, and to arrange for full recovery.
text.Rotherham: if he rise and shall walk abroad on his staff, then shall he that smote him be acquitted,—only, for his loss of time, he shall pay, and, shall surely heal, him.
text.DRB: If he rise again and walk abroad upon his staff, he that struck him shall be quit, yet so that he make restitution for his work, and for his expenses upon the physicians.

text.NRSV: When a slaveowner strikes a male or female slave with a rod and the slave dies immediately, the owner shall be punished.
text.Rotherham: And when a man shall smite his servant or his handmaid with a rod, and he die under his hand, he shall, surely be avenged;
text.DRB: He that striketh his bondman or bondwoman with a rod, and they die under his hands, shall be guilty of the crime.

text.NRSV: But if the slave survives a day or two, there is no punishment; for the slave is the owner’s property.
text.Rotherham: nevertheless if, for a day or for two days, he continue, he shall not be avenged, for, his silver, he is.
text.DRB: But if the party remain alive a day or two, he shall not be subject to the punishment, because it is his money.

text.NRSV: When people who are fighting injure a pregnant woman so that there is a miscarriage, and yet no further harm follows, the one responsible shall be fined what the woman’s husband demands, paying as much as the judges determine.
text.Rotherham: And when men strive together and push against a woman with child, and she miscarry, but there is no other mischief, he shall, surely be fined, according as the woman’s husband shall lay upon him, but he shall give it through judges.
text.DRB: If men quarrel, and one strike a woman with child, and she miscarry indeed, but live herself: he shall be answerable for so much damage as the woman's husband shall require, and as arbiters shall award.

text.NRSV: If any harm follows, then you shall give life for life,
text.Rotherham: But, if mischief follow, then shalt thou give life for life;
text.DRB: But if her death ensue thereupon, he shall render life for life.

text.NRSV: eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
text.Rotherham: eye for eye, tooth for tooth,—hand for hand, foot for foot,
text.DRB: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,

text.NRSV: burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.
text.Rotherham: brand for brand, wound for wound,—stripe for stripe.
text.DRB: Burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.

text.NRSV: When a slaveowner strikes the eye of a male or female slave, destroying it, the owner shall let the slave go, a free person, to compensate for the eye.
text.Rotherham: And, when a man smiteth the eye of his servant, or the eye of his handmaid, and destroyeth it, he shall send him forth, free, for his eye;
text.DRB: If any man strike the eye of his manservant or maidservant, and leave them but one eye, he shall let them go free for the eye which he put out.

text.NRSV: If the owner knocks out a tooth of a male or female slave, the slave shall be let go, a free person, to compensate for the tooth.
text.Rotherham: or, if, the tooth of his servant or the tooth of his handmaid, he knock out, he shall send him forth, free, for his tooth.
text.DRB: Also if he strike out a tooth of his manservant or maidservant, he shall in like manner make them free.

text.NRSV: When an ox gores a man or a woman to death, the ox shall be stoned, and its flesh shall not be eaten; but the owner of the ox shall not be liable.
text.Rotherham: And when an ox goreth a man or a woman, and death ensueth, the ox shall, surely be stoned and his flesh shall not be eaten, and the owner of the ox, shall be quit.
text.DRB: If an ox gore a man or a woman, and they die, he shall be stoned: and his flesh shall not be eaten, but the owner of the ox shall be quit.

text.NRSV: If the ox has been accustomed to gore in the past, and its owner has been warned but has not restrained it, and it kills a man or a woman, the ox shall be stoned, and its owner also shall be put to death.
text.Rotherham: But, if, the ox, was wont to gore before that time and it hath been attested to his owner, and he hath not proceeded to put him under guard, and he causeth the death of man or woman, the ox, shall be stoned, and, his owner also, shall be put to death.
text.DRB: But if the ox was wont to push with his horn yesterday and the day before, and they warned his master, and he did not shut him up, and he shall kill a man or a woman: then the ox shall be stoned, and his owner also shall be put to death.

text.NRSV: If a ransom is imposed on the owner, then the owner shall pay whatever is imposed for the redemption of the victim’s life.
text.Rotherham: If, a sin-covering, be laid on him, then shall he give a ransom for his life, according to whatsoever may be laid on him:
text.DRB: And if they set a price upon him, he shall give for his life whatsoever is laid upon him.

text.NRSV: If it gores a boy or a girl, the owner shall be dealt with according to this same rule.
text.Rotherham: if, a son, he gore or, a daughter, he gore, according to this regulation, shall it be done to him;
text.DRB: If he have gored a son, or a daughter, he shall fall under the like sentence.

text.NRSV: If the ox gores a male or female slave, the owner shall pay to the slaveowner thirty shekels of silver, and the ox shall be stoned.
text.Rotherham: if, a servant the ox gore, or a handmaid, thirty shekels of silver, shall he give to his lord, and, the ox, shall be stoned.
text.DRB: If he assault a bondman or a bond woman, he shall give thirty sicles of silver to their master, and the ox shall be stoned.

text.NRSV: If someone leaves a pit open, or digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or a donkey falls into it,
text.Rotherham: And when a man openeth a pit, or when a man diggeth a pit, and doth not cover it,—and there falleth thereinto an ox or an ass,
text.DRB: If a man open a pit, and dig one, and cover it not, and an ox or an ass fall into it,

text.NRSV: the owner of the pit shall make restitution, giving money to its owner, but keeping the dead animal.
text.Rotherham: the owner of the pit, shall make it good, silver, shall he pay back to the owner thereof,—and the dead beast, shall be his.
text.DRB: The owner of the pit shall pay the price of the beasts: and that which is dead shall be his own.

text.NRSV: If someone’s ox hurts the ox of another, so that it dies, then they shall sell the live ox and divide the money; and the dead animal they shall also divide.
text.Rotherham: And when the ox of one man thrusteth the ox of his neighbour so that it dieth, then shall they sell the live ox and divide the silver thereof, and the dead ox also, shall they divide,
text.DRB: If one man's ox gore another man's ox, and he die: they shall sell the live ox, and shall divide the price, and the carcass of that which died they shall part between them:

text.NRSV: But if it was known that the ox was accustomed to gore in the past, and its owner has not restrained it, the owner shall pay ox for ox, but keep the dead animal.
text.Rotherham: Or if it was known that, an ox, was, wont to gore, before that time, and his owner proceeded not to put him under guard, he shall surely make good,—an ox for the ox, and, the dead one, shall be his.
text.DRB: But if he knew that his ox was wont to push yesterday and the day before, and his master did not keep him in: he shall pay ox for ox, and shall take the whole carcass.

text.NRSV: When someone steals an ox or a sheep, and slaughters it or sells it, the thief shall pay five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep. The thief shall make restitution, but if unable to do so, shall be sold for the theft.
text.Rotherham: When a man stealeth an ox or a sheep, and slayeth it, or selleth it, with five of the herd, shall he make good—for the ox, or with four of the flock, for the sheep.
text.DRB: If any man steal an ox or a sheep, and kill or sell it: he shall restore five oxen for one ox, and four sheep for one sheep.

text.NRSV: If a thief is found breaking in, and is beaten to death, no bloodguilt is incurred;
text.Rotherham: If, in the place of breaking in, the thief be found and he is smitten so that he dieth, there is due for him no shedding of blood:
text.DRB: If a thief be found breaking open a house or undermining it, and be wounded so as to die: he that slew him shall not be guilty of blood.

text.NRSV: but if it happens after sunrise, bloodguilt is incurred.
text.Rotherham: should the sun have arisen upon him, there would be shedding of blood due for him,—he is surely to make restitution, if he hath nothing, then is he to be sold, for his theft.
text.DRB: But if he did this when the sun is risen, he hath committed murder, and he shall die. If he have not wherewith to make restitution for the theft, he shall be sold.

text.NRSV: When the animal, whether ox or donkey or sheep, is found alive in the thief’s possession, the thief shall pay double.
text.Rotherham: If the thing stolen is found in his hand—whether ox or ass or sheep, alive, with two, shall he make restitution.
text.DRB: If that which he stole be found with him, alive, either ox, or ass, or sheep: he shall restore double.

text.NRSV: When someone causes a field or vineyard to be grazed over, or lets livestock loose to graze in someone else’s field, restitution shall be made from the best in the owner’s field or vineyard.
text.Rotherham: When a man causeth a field or a vineyard to be depastured, or hath sent in his own cattle and stripped the field of another, he shall, surely make restitution, out of his own field, according to the yield thereof; or if, all the field, he depasture, with the best of his own field, or with the best of his own vineyard, shall he make restitution.
text.DRB: If any man hurt a field or a vineyard, and put in his beast to feed upon that which is other men's: he shall restore the best of whatsoever he hath in his own field, or in his vineyard, according to the estimation of the damage.

text.NRSV: When fire breaks out and catches in thorns so that the stacked grain or the standing grain or the field is consumed, the one who kindled the fire shall make full restitution.
text.Rotherham: When a fire breaketh out and hath come upon thorns and so there is consumed a stack of sheaves, or the standing corn, or the field, he that kindled the fire, shall surely make restitution.
text.DRB: If a fire breaking out light upon thorns, and catch stacks of corn, or corn standing in the fields, he that kindled the fire shall make good the loss.

text.NRSV: When someone delivers to a neighbor money or goods for safekeeping, and they are stolen from the neighbor’s house, then the thief, if caught, shall pay double.
text.Rotherham: When a man giveth unto his neighbour silver or jewelry, to keep, and it is stolen out of the house of the man:
text.DRB: If a man deliver money, or any vessel unto his friend to keep, and they be stolen away from him that received them: if the thief be found he shall restore double:

text.NRSV: If the thief is not caught, the owner of the house shall be brought before God, to determine whether or not the owner had laid hands on the neighbor’s goods.
text.Rotherham: if the thief be found, he shall give in restitution double, if the thief be not found, then shall the owner of the house be brought near unto God to swear that he hath not laid his hand on the property of his neighbour.
text.DRB: If the thief be not known, the master of the house shall be brought to the gods, and shall swear that he did not lay his hand upon his neighbour's goods,

text.NRSV: In any case of disputed ownership involving ox, donkey, sheep, clothing, or any other loss, of which one party says, “This is mine,” the case of both parties shall come before God; the one whom God condemns shall pay double to the other.
text.Rotherham: For any affair of trespass—for an ox, for an ass, for a sheep, for a mantle for anything lost as to which one could say—This is it, unto God, shall come the affair of them both,—he whom God shall condemn, shall make restitution of double to his neighbour.
text.DRB: To do any fraud, either in ox, or in ass, or sheep, or raiment, or any thing that may bring damage: the cause of both parties shall come to the gods: and if they give judgment, he shall restore double to his neighbour.

text.NRSV: When someone delivers to another a donkey, ox, sheep, or any other animal for safekeeping, and it dies or is maimed or is carried off, without anyone seeing it,
text.Rotherham: When a man delivereth unto his neighbour, an ass, or an ox or a sheep, or any beast, to keep,—and it dieth or is fractured, or is driven away, no one beholding,
text.DRB: If a man deliver ass, ox, sheep, or any beast, to his neighbour's custody, and it die, or be hurt, or be taken by enemies, and no man saw it:

text.NRSV: an oath before the Lord shall decide between the two of them that the one has not laid hands on the property of the other; the owner shall accept the oath, and no restitution shall be made.
text.Rotherham: the oath of Yahweh, shall come between them both, That he hath not laid his hand on the property of his neighbour,—then shall the owner thereof accept it and he shall not make restitution.
text.DRB: There shall be an oath between them, that he did not put forth his hand to his neighbour's goods: and the owner shall accept of the oath; and he shall not be compelled to make restitution.

text.NRSV: But if it was stolen, restitution shall be made to its owner.
text.Rotherham: But, if it be verily stolen, from him, he shall make restitution to the owner thereof.
text.DRB: But if it were taken away by stealth, he shall make the loss good to the owner.

text.NRSV: If it was mangled by beasts, let it be brought as evidence; restitution shall not be made for the mangled remains.
text.Rotherham: If it be, verily torn in pieces, he shall bring it in as a witness—for that which was torn, he shall not make restitution.
text.DRB: If it were eaten by a beast, let him bring to him that which was slain, and he shall not make restitution.

text.NRSV: When someone borrows an animal from another and it is injured or dies, the owner not being present, full restitution shall be made.
text.Rotherham: And when a man asketh aught of his neighbour, and it is fractured or dieth, its owner, not being with it, he shall, surely make restitution.
text.DRB: If a man borrow of his neighbour any of these things, and it be hurt or die, the owner not being present, he shall be obliged to make restitution.

text.NRSV: If the owner was present, there shall be no restitution; if it was hired, only the hiring fee is due.
text.Rotherham: If, its owner, was with it, he shall not make restitution,—if it is, hired, it cometh into its hire.
text.DRB: But if the owner be present, he shall not make restitution, especially if it were hired and came for the hire of his work.

text.NRSV: When a man seduces a virgin who is not engaged to be married, and lies with her, he shall give the bride-price for her and make her his wife.
text.Rotherham: And, when a man enticeth a virgin who is not betrothed, and lieth with her, he shall, surely pay a purchase-price, that she may be his wife.
text.DRB: If a man seduce a virgin not yet espoused, and lie with her: he shall endow her, and have her to wife.

text.NRSV: But if her father refuses to give her to him, he shall pay an amount equal to the bride-price for virgins.
text.Rotherham: If her father utterly refuse, to give her to him, silver, shall he weigh out, according to the purchase-price of virgins.
text.DRB: If the maid's father will not give her to him, he shall give money according to the dowry, which virgins are wont to receive.

text.NRSV: You shall not permit a female sorcerer to live.
text.Rotherham: A sorceress, shalt thou not suffer to live.
text.DRB: Wizards thou shalt not suffer to live.

text.NRSV: Whoever lies with an animal shall be put to death.
text.Rotherham: Whosoever lieth with a beast shall surely be put to death.
text.DRB: Whosoever copulateth with a beast shall be put to death.

text.NRSV: Whoever sacrifices to any god, other than the Lord alone, shall be devoted to destruction.
text.Rotherham: He that sacrificeth to the gods shall be devoted to destruction,—except [he sacrificeth] to Yahweh alone.
text.DRB: He that sacrificeth to gods, shall be put to death, save only to the Lord.

text.NRSV: You shall not wrong or oppress a resident alien, for you were aliens in the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And, a sojourner, shalt thou not tread down neither shalt thou drive him away,—for sojourners, ye became in the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not molest a stranger, nor afflict him: for yourselves also were strangers in the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: You shall not abuse any widow or orphan.
text.Rotherham: Neither widow nor fatherless, shalt thou humiliate:
text.DRB: You shall not hurt a widow or an orphan.

text.NRSV: If you do abuse them, when they cry out to me, I will surely heed their cry;
text.Rotherham: if thou, do humiliate, him, when he in anywise crieth out unto me, I will, surely hear, his outcry:
text.DRB: If you hurt them they will cry out to me, and I will hear their cry:

text.NRSV: my wrath will burn, and I will kill you with the sword, and your wives shall become widows and your children orphans.
text.Rotherham: so shall kindle mine anger, and I will slay you with the sword,—and your wives, shall become widows, and your, sons fatherless.
text.DRB: And my rage shall be enkindled, and I will strike you with the sword, and your wives shall be widows, and your children fatherless.

text.NRSV: If you lend money to my people, to the poor among you, you shall not deal with them as a creditor; you shall not exact interest from them.
text.Rotherham: If, silver, thou wilt lend unto my people—unto the humbled one by thee, thou shalt not be to him like one that lendeth on interest,—thou shalt not lay upon him interest.
text.DRB: If thou lend money to any of my people that is poor, that dwelleth with thee, thou shalt not be hard upon them as an extortioner, nor oppress them with usuries.

text.NRSV: If you take your neighbor’s cloak in pawn, you shall restore it before the sun goes down;
text.Rotherham: If thou, do take in pledge, the mantle of thy neighbour, by the going in of the sun, shalt thou restore it to him;
text.DRB: If thou take of thy neighbour a garment in pledge, thou shalt give it him again before sunset.

text.NRSV: for it may be your neighbor’s only clothing to use as cover; in what else shall that person sleep? And if your neighbor cries out to me, I will listen, for I am compassionate.
text.Rotherham: for that is his only covering, that, is his mantle, for his skin,—wherein shall he sleep? and it shall come to pass when he maketh outcry unto me, then will I hear because, gracious, I am.
text.DRB: For that same is the only thing wherewith he is covered, the clothing of his body, neither hath he any other to sleep in: if he cry to me, I will hear him, because I am compassionate.

text.NRSV: You shall not revile God, nor curse a ruler of your people.
text.Rotherham: God, shalt thou not revile,—and a prince among thy people, shalt thou not curse.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not speak ill of the gods, and the prince of thy people thou shalt not curse.

text.NRSV: You shall not delay to make offerings from the fullness of your harvest and from the outflow of your presses. The firstborn of your sons you shall give to me.
text.Rotherham: Thy fulness and thine outflow, shalt thou not delay,—the firstborn of thy sons, shalt thou give to me.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not delay to pay thy tithes and thy firstfruits: thou shalt give the firstborn of thy sons to me.

text.NRSV: You shall do the same with your oxen and with your sheep: seven days it shall remain with its mother; on the eighth day you shall give it to me.
text.Rotherham: Thus, shalt thou do with thine ox with thy sheep,—seven days, shall it be with its dam, on the eighth day, shalt thou give it to me.
text.DRB: Thou shalt do the same with the firstborn of thy oxen also and sheep: seven days let it be with its dam, the eighth day thou shalt give it to me.

text.NRSV: You shall be people consecrated to me; therefore you shall not eat any meat that is mangled by beasts in the field; you shall throw it to the dogs.
text.Rotherham: Holy men, therefore shall ye truly become to me,—and flesh in the field torn to pieces, shall ye not eat, to the dogs, shall ye cast it.
text.DRB: You shall be holy men to me: the flesh that beasts have tasted of before, you shall not eat, but shall cast it to the dogs.

text.NRSV: You shall not spread a false report. You shall not join hands with the wicked to act as a malicious witness.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not utter a false report. Do not lay thy hand with a lawless man, to become a witness helping forward violence and wrong.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not receive the voice of a lie: neither shalt thou join thy hand to bear false witness for a wicked person.

text.NRSV: You shall not follow a majority in wrongdoing; when you bear witness in a lawsuit, you shall not side with the majority so as to pervert justice;
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not follow multitudes to commit wickedness,—neither shalt thou answer in a quarrel, so as to turn away after multitudes to mislead,
text.DRB: Thou shalt not follow the multitude to do evil: neither shalt thou yield in judgment, to the opinion of the most part, to stray from the truth.

text.NRSV: nor shall you be partial to the poor in a lawsuit.
text.Rotherham: Even a poor man, shalt thou not prefer in his quarrel.
text.DRB: Neither shalt thou favour a poor man in judgment.

text.NRSV: When you come upon your enemy’s ox or donkey going astray, you shall bring it back.
text.Rotherham: When thou meetest the ox of thine enemy, or his ass, going astray, thou shalt, surely bring it back, to him.
text.DRB: If thou meet thy enemy's ox or ass going astray, bring it back to him.

text.NRSV: When you see the donkey of one who hates you lying under its burden and you would hold back from setting it free, you must help to set it free.
text.Rotherham: When thou seest the ass of him that hateth thee sinking down under his burden, and mightest have foreborne to unload him, thou shalt surely help him to unload,
text.DRB: If thou see the ass of him that hateth thee lie underneath his burden, thou shalt not pass by, but shalt lift him up with him.

text.NRSV: You shall not pervert the justice due to your poor in their lawsuits.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not pervert the vindication of thy needy one in his quarrel.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not go aside in the poor man's judgment.

text.NRSV: Keep far from a false charge, and do not kill the innocent and those in the right, for I will not acquit the guilty.
text.Rotherham: From a matter of falsehood, thou shalt keep far away,—and the innocent one and the righteous, do not thou slay, for I will not justify a lawless man.
text.DRB: Thou shalt fly lying. The innocent and just person thou shalt not put to death: because I abhor the wicked.

text.NRSV: You shall take no bribe, for a bribe blinds the officials, and subverts the cause of those who are in the right.
text.Rotherham: And, a bribe, shalt thou not take,—for the bribe, blindeth the clear-sighted, and perverteth the words of the righteous.
text.DRB: Neither shalt thou take bribes, which even blind the wise, and pervert the words of the just.

text.NRSV: You shall not oppress a resident alien; you know the heart of an alien, for you were aliens in the land of Egypt.
text.Rotherham: And a sojourner, shalt thou not drive away,—seeing that, ye yourselves, know the, soul of a sojourner; for sojourners, became ye in the land of Egypt.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not molest a stranger, for you know the hearts of strangers: for you also were strangers in the land of Egypt.

text.NRSV: For six years you shall sow your land and gather in its yield;
text.Rotherham: And six years, shalt thou sow thy land,—and shalt gather the yield thereof;
text.DRB: Six years thou shalt sow thy ground, and shalt gather the corn thereof.

text.NRSV: but the seventh year you shall let it rest and lie fallow, so that the poor of your people may eat; and what they leave the wild animals may eat. You shall do the same with your vineyard, and with your olive grove.
text.Rotherham: but the seventh year, shalt thou let it rest and be still so shall the needy of thy people eat, and what they leave, shall the wild-beast of the field eat,—in like manner, shalt thou deal with thy vineyard with thine oliveyard.
text.DRB: But the seventh year thou shalt let it alone, and suffer it to rest, that the poor of thy people may eat, and whatsoever shall be left, let the beasts of the field eat it: so shalt thou do with thy vineyard and thy oliveyard.

text.NRSV: Six days you shall do your work, but on the seventh day you shall rest, so that your ox and your donkey may have relief, and your homeborn slave and the resident alien may be refreshed.
text.Rotherham: Six days, shalt thou do thy work, but on the seventh day, shalt thou keep sabbath,—that thine ox may rest and thine ass, and that the son of thy handmaid and the sojourner may be refreshed.
text.DRB: Six days thou shalt work: the seventh day thou shalt cease, that thy ox and thy ass may rest: and the son of thy handmaid and the stranger may be refreshed.

text.NRSV: Be attentive to all that I have said to you. Do not invoke the names of other gods; do not let them be heard on your lips.
text.Rotherham: And, in all that I have said unto thee, shalt thou take heed to thyself,—and the name of other gods, shalt thou not mention, it shall not be heard upon thy mouth.
text.DRB: Keep all things that I have said to you. And by the name of strange gods you shall not swear, neither shall it be heard out of your mouth.

text.NRSV: Three times in the year you shall hold a festival for me.
text.Rotherham: Three times, shalt thou keep festival to me, in the year.
text.DRB: Three times every year you shall celebrate feasts to me.

text.NRSV: You shall observe the festival of unleavened bread; as I commanded you, you shall eat unleavened bread for seven days at the appointed time in the month of Abib, for in it you came out of Egypt. No one shall appear before me empty-handed.
text.Rotherham: The festival of unleavened cakes, shalt thou keep,—seven days, shalt thou eat unleavened cakes, as I commanded thee at the appointed time of the month Abib; for, therein, camest thou forth out of Egypt,—and they shall not see my face, empty-handed.
text.DRB: Thou shalt keep the feast of unleavened bread. Seven days shalt thou eat unleavened bread, as I commanded thee, in the time of the month of new corn, when thou didst come forth out of Egypt: thou shalt not appear empty before me.

text.NRSV: You shall observe the festival of harvest, of the first fruits of your labor, of what you sow in the field. You shall observe the festival of ingathering at the end of the year, when you gather in from the field the fruit of your labor.
text.Rotherham: And the festival of harvest with the firstfruits of thy labours, which thou shalt sow in the field, And the festival of ingathering—at the outgoing of the year, when thou hast gathered in thy labours out of the field.
text.DRB: And the feast of the harvest of the firstfruits of thy work, whatsoever thou hast sown in the field. The feast also in the end of the year, when thou hast gathered in all thy corn out of the field.

text.NRSV: Three times in the year all your males shall appear before the Lord God.
text.Rotherham: Three times in the year, shall all thy males see the face of the Lord, Yahweh.
text.DRB: Thrice a year shall all thy males appear before the Lord thy God.

text.NRSV: You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with anything leavened, or let the fat of my festival remain until the morning.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not offer with anything leavened, the blood of my sacrifice,—neither shall the fat of my festival-sacrifice remain until morning.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not sacrifice the blood of my victim upon leaven, neither shall the fat of my solemnity remain until the morning.

text.NRSV: The choice first fruits of your ground you shall bring into the house of the Lord your God. You shall not boil a kid in its mother’s milk.
text.Rotherham: The beginning of the firstfruits of thy ground, shalt thou bring into the house of Yahweh thy God. Thou shalt not boil a kid in the milk of its dam.
text.DRB: Thou shalt carry the firstfruits of the corn of thy ground to the house of the Lord thy God. Thou shalt not boil a kid in the milk of his dam.

text.NRSV: I am going to send an angel in front of you, to guard you on the way and to bring you to the place that I have prepared.
text.Rotherham: Lo! I, am sending a messenger before thee, to guard thee in the way,—and to bring thee into the place which I have prepared.
text.DRB: Behold I will send my angel, who shall go before thee, and keep thee in thy journey, and bring thee into the place that I have prepared.

text.NRSV: Be attentive to him and listen to his voice; do not rebel against him, for he will not pardon your transgression; for my name is in him.
text.Rotherham: Take thou heed of his presence, and hearken to his voice—do not vex him,-for he will not pardon your transgression, for, my name, is within him.
text.DRB: Take notice of him, and hear his voice, and do not think him one to be contemned: for he will not forgive when thou hast sinned, and my name is in him.

text.NRSV: But if you listen attentively to his voice and do all that I say, then I will be an enemy to your enemies and a foe to your foes.
text.Rotherham: But if thou wilt indeed hearken, to his voice, and so wilt do all that I may speak, then will I be an enemy to thine enemies, and an adversary to thine adversaries;
text.DRB: But if thou wilt hear his voice, and do all that I speak, I will be an enemy to thy enemies, and will afflict them that afflict thee.

text.NRSV: When my angel goes in front of you, and brings you to the Amorites, the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Canaanites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites, and I blot them out,
text.Rotherham: for my messenger shall go before thee, and bring thee in—unto the Amorite and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Canaanite, the Hivite and the Jebusite,—so will I destroy them.
text.DRB: And my angel shall go before thee, and shall bring thee in unto the Amorrhite, and the Hethite, and the Pherezite, and the Chanaanite, and the Hevite, and the Jebusite, whom I will destroy.

text.NRSV: you shall not bow down to their gods, or worship them, or follow their practices, but you shall utterly demolish them and break their pillars in pieces.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not bow thyself down to their gods neither shalt thou be led to serve them, neither shalt thou do according to their works,—but thou shalt, verily overthrow, them, and, completely break in pieces, their pillars.
text.DRB: Thou shalt not adore their gods, nor serve them. Thou shalt not do their works, but shalt destroy them, and break their statues.

text.NRSV: You shall worship the Lord your God, and I will bless your bread and your water; and I will take sickness away from among you.
text.Rotherham: So shall ye serve Yahweh your God, and he will bless thy bread and thy water,—and I will take away sickness out of thy midst.
text.DRB: And you shall serve the Lord your God, that I may bless your bread and your waters, and may take away sickness from the midst of thee.

text.NRSV: No one shall miscarry or be barren in your land; I will fulfill the number of your days.
text.Rotherham: There shall be nothing casting its young or barren in thy land,—the number of thy days, will I make full.
text.DRB: There shall not be one fruitless nor barren in thy land: I will fill the number of thy days.

text.NRSV: I will send my terror in front of you, and will throw into confusion all the people against whom you shall come, and I will make all your enemies turn their backs to you.
text.Rotherham: My terror, will I send before thee, and will confound all the people amongst whom thou shalt come, and will deliver up all thine enemies unto thee as they flee.
text.DRB: I will send my fear before thee, and will destroy all the people to whom thou shalt come: and will turn the backs of all thy enemies before thee.

text.NRSV: And I will send the pestilence in front of you, which shall drive out the Hivites, the Canaanites, and the Hittites from before you.
text.Rotherham: And I will send the hornet, before thee,—and it shall drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite, from before thee.
text.DRB: Sending out hornets before, that shall drive away the Hevite, and the Chanaanite, and the Hethite, before thou come in.

text.NRSV: I will not drive them out from before you in one year, or the land would become desolate and the wild animals would multiply against you.
text.Rotherham: I will not drive them out from before thee, in one year,—lest the land should become a desolation, so would the wild-beast of the field multiply over thee.
text.DRB: I will not cast them out from thy face in one year: lest the land be brought into a wilderness, and the beasts multiply against thee.

text.NRSV: Little by little I will drive them out from before you, until you have increased and possess the land.
text.Rotherham: Little by little, will I drive them out from before thee. until thou become fruitful, then shalt thou take possession of the land.
text.DRB: By little and little I will drive them out from before thee, till thou be increased, and dost possess the land.

text.NRSV: I will set your borders from the Red Sea to the sea of the Philistines, and from the wilderness to the Euphrates; for I will hand over to you the inhabitants of the land, and you shall drive them out before you.
text.Rotherham: So will I set thy bounds from the Red Sea even unto the sea of the Philistines and from the desert unto the River (Euphrates),—for I will deliver into your hand the inhabitants of the land, so shalt thou drive them out from before thee.
text.DRB: And I will set thy bounds from the Red Sea to the sea of the Palestines, and from the desert to the river: I will deliver the inhabitants of the land into your hands, and will drive them out from before you.

text.NRSV: You shall make no covenant with them and their gods.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt not make with them or with their gods, a covenant:
text.DRB: Thou shalt not enter into league with them, nor with their gods.

text.NRSV: They shall not live in your land, or they will make you sin against me; for if you worship their gods, it will surely be a snare to you.
text.Rotherham: they shall not dwell in thy land, lest they cause thee to sin against me,—for thou mightest serve their gods, verily! it would become to thee a snare!
text.DRB: Let them not dwell in thy land, lest perhaps they make thee sin against me, if thou serve their gods: which undoubtedly will be a scandal to thee.

text.NRSV: Then he said to Moses, “Come up to the Lord, with Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel, and worship at a distance.
text.Rotherham: And unto Moses, he said—Come up unto Yahweh—thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel, so shall ye bow yourselves down from afar.

text.NRSV: Moses alone shall come near the Lord; but the others shall not come near, and the people shall not come up with him.”
text.Rotherham: And Moses alone shall draw near unto Yahweh, but, they shall not draw near, and, the people, shall not come up with him.

text.NRSV: Moses came and told the people all the words of the Lord and all the ordinances; and all the people answered with one voice, and said, “All the words that the Lord has spoken we will do.”
text.Rotherham: So Moses came, and recounted to the people all the words of Yahweh, and all the regulations, and all the people responded with one voice and said, All the words which Yahweh hath spoken, will we do.

text.NRSV: And Moses wrote down all the words of the Lord. He rose early in the morning, and built an altar at the foot of the mountain, and set up twelve pillars, corresponding to the twelve tribes of Israel.
text.Rotherham: Then wrote Moses all the words of Yahweh, and rose up early in the morning, and builded an altar, under the mountain, and twelve pillars, for the twelve tribes of Israel:

text.NRSV: He sent young men of the people of Israel, who offered burnt offerings and sacrificed oxen as offerings of well-being to the Lord.
text.Rotherham: and he sent young men of the sons of Israel, and they caused to go up, ascending-sacrifices,—and slew peace-offerings to Yahweh, of oxen.

text.NRSV: Moses took half of the blood and put it in basins, and half of the blood he dashed against the altar.
text.Rotherham: Then took Moses half of the blood, and put it in basins, and half of the blood, dashed he over the altar:

text.NRSV: Then he took the book of the covenant, and read it in the hearing of the people; and they said, “All that the Lord has spoken we will do, and we will be obedient.”
text.Rotherham: then took he the book of the covenant, and read in the ears of the people. And they said, All that Yahweh hath spoken, will we do, and will hearken,

text.NRSV: Moses took the blood and dashed it on the people, and said, “See the blood of the covenant that the Lord has made with you in accordance with all these words.”
text.Rotherham: Then Moses took the blood, and dashed over the people, and said, Lo! the blood of the covenant which Yahweh hath solemnised with you, over all these words.

text.NRSV: Then Moses and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel went up,
text.Rotherham: Then went up Moses and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel;

text.NRSV: and they saw the God of Israel. Under his feet there was something like a pavement of sapphire stone, like the very heaven for clearness.
text.Rotherham: and they saw the God of Israel,—and under his feet, like a pavement of sapphire, and like the very heavens for brightness; and

text.NRSV: God did not lay his hand on the chief men of the people of Israel; also they beheld God, and they ate and drank.
text.Rotherham: against the nobles of the sons of Israel, put he not forth his hand,—so then they had vision of God, and did eat and drink,

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Moses, “Come up to me on the mountain, and wait there; and I will give you the tablets of stone, with the law and the commandment, which I have written for their instruction.”
text.Rotherham: Then said Yahweh unto Moses—Come thou up unto me in the mountain and remain thou there,—for I must give thee tables of stone and the law and the commandment, which I have written to direct them.

text.NRSV: So Moses set out with his assistant Joshua, and Moses went up into the mountain of God.
text.Rotherham: And Moses rose up, and Joshua his attendant, and Moses went up into the mountain of God;

text.NRSV: To the elders he had said, “Wait here for us, until we come to you again; for Aaron and Hur are with you; whoever has a legal dispute can go to them.”
text.Rotherham: but, unto the elders, he said—Tarry for us here, until we return unto you. And lo! Aaron and Hur, are with you, he that hath a cause, let him draw near unto them.

text.NRSV: Then Moses went up on the mountain, and the cloud covered the mountain.
text.Rotherham: So then Moses wont up into the mountain,—and the cloud covered the mountain.

text.NRSV: The glory of the Lord settled on Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it for six days; on the seventh day he called to Moses out of the cloud.
text.Rotherham: And the glory of Yahweh rested upon Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days,—then called he unto Moses on the seventh day, out of the midst of the cloud.

text.NRSV: Now the appearance of the glory of the Lord was like a devouring fire on the top of the mountain in the sight of the people of Israel.
text.Rotherham: And the appearance of the glory of Yahweh was like a consuming fire on the top of the mountain,—in the sight of the sons of Israel.

text.NRSV: Moses entered the cloud, and went up on the mountain. Moses was on the mountain for forty days and forty nights.
text.Rotherham: And Moses entered into the midst of the cloud and ascended into the mountain. And it came to pass that Moses was in the mountain forty days and forty nights.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Moses:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh spake unto Moses, saying:
text.DRB: And the Lord spoke to Moses, saying:

text.NRSV: Tell the Israelites to take for me an offering; from all whose hearts prompt them to give you shall receive the offering for me.
text.Rotherham: Speak unto the sons of Israel, that they take for me a heave-offering,—of every man whose heart urgeth him, shall ye take my heave-offering.
text.DRB: Speak to the children of Israel, that they bring firstfruits to me: of every man that offereth of his own accord, you shall take them.

text.NRSV: This is the offering that you shall receive from them: gold, silver, and bronze,
text.Rotherham: And, this is the heave-offering which ye shall take of them,—gold and silver and bronze;
text.DRB: And these are the things you must take: gold, and silver, and brass,

text.NRSV: blue, purple, and crimson yarns and fine linen, goats’ hair,
text.Rotherham: and blue, and purple, and crimson and fine linen, and goat’s hair;
text.DRB: Violet and purple, and scarlet twice dyed, and fine linen, and goats' hair,

text.NRSV: tanned rams’ skins, fine leather, acacia wood,
text.Rotherham: and rams’ skins dyed red, and badgers’ skins and acacia wood;
text.DRB: And rams' skins dyed red, and violet skins, and setim wood :

text.NRSV: oil for the lamps, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense,
text.Rotherham: oil for giving light,—perfumes for the anointing oil, and for fragrant incense;
text.DRB: Oil to make lights: spices for ointment, and for sweetsmelling incense:

text.NRSV: onyx stones and gems to be set in the ephod and for the breastpiece.
text.Rotherham: onyx stones, and setting stones,—for the ephod and for the breastpiece:
text.DRB: Onyx stones, and precious stones to adorn the ephod and the rational .

text.NRSV: And have them make me a sanctuary, so that I may dwell among them.
text.Rotherham: So shall they make for me a sanctuary,—and I will make my habitation in their midst:
text.DRB: And they shall make me a sanctuary, and I will dwell in the midst of them:

text.NRSV: In accordance with all that I show you concerning the pattern of the tabernacle and of all its furniture, so you shall make it.
text.Rotherham: according to all that, I, am shewing thee, the pattern of the habitation, and the pattern of all the furnishings thereof, even so, shall ye make it.
text.DRB: According to all the likeness of the tabernacle which I will shew thee, and of all the vessels for the service thereof: and thus you shall make it:

text.NRSV: They shall make an ark of acacia wood; it shall be two and a half cubits long, a cubit and a half wide, and a cubit and a half high.
text.Rotherham: And they shall make an ark of acacia wood,-two cubits and a half, the length thereof and, a cubit and a half, the breadth thereof, and, a cubit and a half, the height thereof.
text.DRB: Frame an ark of setim wood, the length whereof shall be of two cubits and a half: the breadth, a cubit and a half: the height, likewise, a cubit and a half.

text.NRSV: You shall overlay it with pure gold, inside and outside you shall overlay it, and you shall make a molding of gold upon it all around.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt overlay it with pure gold, within and without, shalt thou overlay it,—and thou shalt make thereon a rim of gold, round about.
text.DRB: And thou shalt overlay it with the purest gold within and without: and over it thou shalt make a golden crown round about:

text.NRSV: You shall cast four rings of gold for it and put them on its four feet, two rings on the one side of it, and two rings on the other side.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt cast for it four rings of gold, and shalt put them on the four feet thereof,—even two rings on the one side thereof, and two rings on the other side thereof.
text.DRB: And four golden rings, which thou shall put at the four corners of the ark: let two rings be on the one side, and two on the other.

text.NRSV: You shall make poles of acacia wood, and overlay them with gold.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make staves, of acacia wood,—and shalt overlay them with gold.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make bars also of setim wood, and shalt overlay them with gold.

text.NRSV: And you shall put the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark, by which to carry the ark.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt bring in the staves into the rings, on the sides of the ark,—to bear the ark therewith,
text.DRB: And thou shalt put them in through the rings that are in the sides of the ark, that it may be carried on them.

text.NRSV: The poles shall remain in the rings of the ark; they shall not be taken from it.
text.Rotherham: In the rings of the ark, shall remain the staves,—they shall not be removed therefrom.
text.DRB: And they shall be always in the rings, neither shall they at any time be drawn out of them.

text.NRSV: You shall put into the ark the covenant that I shall give you.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt put into the ark,—the testimony which I will give unto thee.
text.DRB: And thou shalt put in the ark the testimony which I will give thee.

text.NRSV: Then you shall make a mercy seat of pure gold; two cubits and a half shall be its length, and a cubit and a half its width.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make a propitiatory, of pure gold,—two cubits and a half, the length thereof, and, a cubit and a half, the breadth thereof,
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also a propitiatory of the purest gold: the length thereof shall be two cubits and a half, and the breadth a cubit and a half.

text.NRSV: You shall make two cherubim of gold; you shall make them of hammered work, at the two ends of the mercy seat.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make two cherubim of gold,—of beaten work, shalt thou make them, out of the two ends of the propitiatory.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also two cherubims of beaten gold, on the two sides of the oracle.

text.NRSV: Make one cherub at the one end, and one cherub at the other; of one piece with the mercy seat you shall make the cherubim at its two ends.
text.Rotherham: And make thou one cherub out of this end, and the other cherub out of that end,—out of the propitiatory, shall ye make the cherubim upon the two ends thereof.
text.DRB: Let one cherub be on the one side, and the other on the other.

text.NRSV: The cherubim shall spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat with their wings. They shall face one to another; the faces of the cherubim shall be turned toward the mercy seat.
text.Rotherham: And the cherubim shall be spreading out two wings on high—making a shelter with their wings over the propitiatory, with their faces one towards the other,—towards the propitiatory, shall be the faces of the cherubim.
text.DRB: Let them cover both sides of the propitiatory, spreading their wings, and covering the oracle, and let them look one towards the other, their faces being turned towards the propitiatory wherewith the ark is to be covered.

text.NRSV: You shall put the mercy seat on the top of the ark; and in the ark you shall put the covenant that I shall give you.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt put the propitiatory upon the ark above,—and in the ark, shalt thou put the testimony, which I will give unto thee.
text.DRB: In which thou shalt put the testimony that I will give thee.

text.NRSV: There I will meet with you, and from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim that are on the ark of the covenant, I will deliver to you all my commands for the Israelites.
text.Rotherham: And I will meet with thee there, and will speak with thee from off the propitiatory, from between the two cherubim, which are upon the ark of the testimony,—whatsoever I may give thee in charge, for the sons of Israel.
text.DRB: Thence will I give orders, and will speak to thee over the propitiatory, and from the midst of the two cherubims, which shall be upon the ark of the testimony, all things which I will command the children of Israel by thee.

text.NRSV: You shall make a table of acacia wood, two cubits long, one cubit wide, and a cubit and a half high.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make a table, of acacia wood, two cubits, the length thereof, and a cubit the breadth thereof, and a cubit and a half the height thereof.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make a table also of setim wood, of two cubits in length, and a cubit in breadth, and a cubit and half in height.

text.NRSV: You shall overlay it with pure gold, and make a molding of gold around it.
text.Rotherham: and thou shalt overlay it with pure gold,—and shalt make thereto a rim of gold, round about;
text.DRB: And thou shalt overlay it with the purest gold: and thou shalt make to it a golden ledge round about.

text.NRSV: You shall make around it a rim a handbreadth wide, and a molding of gold around the rim.
text.Rotherham: and thou shalt make thereto a border of a handbreadth, round about,—and shalt make a rim of gold to the border thereof, round about;
text.DRB: And to the ledge itself a polished crown, four inches high: and over the same another little golden crown.

text.NRSV: You shall make for it four rings of gold, and fasten the rings to the four corners at its four legs.
text.Rotherham: and thou shalt make to it four rings of gold,—and shalt place the rings on the four corners which pertain to the four feet thereof:
text.DRB: Thou shalt prepare also four golden rings, and shalt put them in the four corners of the same table over each foot.

text.NRSV: The rings that hold the poles used for carrying the table shall be close to the rim.
text.Rotherham: adjoining the border, shall be the rings,—as receptacles for the staves, for lifting the table;
text.DRB: Under the crown shall the golden rings be, that the bars may be put through them, and the table may be carried.

text.NRSV: You shall make the poles of acacia wood, and overlay them with gold, and the table shall be carried with these.
text.Rotherham: and thou shalt make the staves of acacia wood, and shalt overlay them with gold,—and the table shall be lifted there, with;
text.DRB: The bars also themselves thou shalt make of setim wood, and shalt overlay them with gold to bear up the table.

text.NRSV: You shall make its plates and dishes for incense, and its flagons and bowls with which to pour drink offerings; you shall make them of pure gold.
text.Rotherham: and thou shalt make its dishes and its pans and its basins and its bowls, for pouring out therewith,—of pure gold, shalt thou make them.
text.DRB: Thou shalt prepare also dishes, and bowls, censers, and cups, wherein the libations are to be offered of the purest gold.

text.NRSV: And you shall set the bread of the Presence on the table before me always.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt place upon the table Presence-bread, before me continually.
text.DRB: And thou shalt set upon the table loaves of proposition in my sight always.

text.NRSV: You shall make a lampstand of pure gold. The base and the shaft of the lampstand shall be made of hammered work; its cups, its calyxes, and its petals shall be of one piece with it;
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make a lampstand of pure gold, of beaten work, shall the lampstand be made, even its base and its shaft its cups, its apples, and its blossoms of the same, shall be:
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also a candlestick of beaten work of the finest gold, the shaft thereof, and the branches, the cups, and the bowls, and the lilies going forth from it.

text.NRSV: and there shall be six branches going out of its sides, three branches of the lampstand out of one side of it and three branches of the lampstand out of the other side of it;
text.Rotherham: with, six branches, coming out of its sides, three branches of the lampstand, out the one side thereof, and three branches of the lampstand, out of the other side thereof:
text.DRB: Six branches shall come out of the sides, three out of the one side, and three out of the other.

text.NRSV: three cups shaped like almond blossoms, each with calyx and petals, on one branch, and three cups shaped like almond blossoms, each with calyx and petals, on the other branch—so for the six branches going out of the lampstand.
text.Rotherham: three cups like almond-flowers, in the one branch apple and blossom, and, three cups like almond-flowers, in the other branch apple and blossom, so, for the six branches, coming out of the lampstand.
text.DRB: Three cups as it were nuts to every branch, and a bowl withal, and a lily; and three cups, likewise of the fashion of nuts in the other branch, and a bowl withal, and a lily. Such shall be the work of the six branches, that are to come out from the shaft:

text.NRSV: On the lampstand itself there shall be four cups shaped like almond blossoms, each with its calyxes and petals.
text.Rotherham: And in the lampstand itself, four cups,—like almond-flowers—its apples, and its blossoms;
text.DRB: And in the candlestick itself shall be four cups in the manner of a nut, and at every one, bowls and lilies.

text.NRSV: There shall be a calyx of one piece with it under the first pair of branches, a calyx of one piece with it under the next pair of branches, and a calyx of one piece with it under the last pair of branches—so for the six branches going out of it.
text.Rotherham: and an apple under two branches of the same, and, another apple, under two other branches of the same, and another apple under two other branches, of the same,—for the six branches, coming out of the lampstand:
text.DRB: Bowls under two branches in three places, which together make six coming forth out of one shaft.

text.NRSV: Their calyxes and their branches shall be of one piece with it, the whole of it one hammered piece of pure gold.
text.Rotherham: their apples and their branches, of the same, shall be,—all of it, of one beaten work, of pure gold.
text.DRB: And both the bowls and the branches shall be of the same beaten work of the purest gold.

text.NRSV: You shall make the seven lamps for it; and the lamps shall be set up so as to give light on the space in front of it.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make the lamps thereof seven, and one shall light up its lamps, and it shall give light, over against the face thereof.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also seven lamps, and shalt set them upon the candlestick, to give light over against.

text.NRSV: Its snuffers and trays shall be of pure gold.
text.Rotherham: And, its snuffers, and its snuff-trays of pure gold.
text.DRB: The snuffers also and where the snuffings shall be put out, shall be made of the purest gold.

text.NRSV: It, and all these utensils, shall be made from a talent of pure gold.
text.Rotherham: With a talent of pure gold, shall he make it, with all these vessels,
text.DRB: The whole weight of the candlestick with all the furniture thereof shall be a talent of the purest gold.

text.NRSV: And see that you make them according to the pattern for them, which is being shown you on the mountain.
text.Rotherham: And see thou and make them, by the pattern of them, which thou wast caused to behold in the mount
text.DRB: Look and make it according to the pattern, that was shewn thee in the mount.

text.NRSV: Moreover you shall make the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine twisted linen, and blue, purple, and crimson yarns; you shall make them with cherubim skillfully worked into them.
text.Rotherham: Also the habitation shalt thou make, with ten curtains, of twined linen, and blue, and purple, and crimson, with cherubim, the work of a skilful weaver, shalt thou make them.
text.DRB: And thou shalt make the tabernacle in this manner: Thou shalt make ten curtains of fine twisted linen, and violet and purple, and scarlet twice dyed, diversified with embroidery.

text.NRSV: The length of each curtain shall be twenty-eight cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits; all the curtains shall be of the same size.
text.Rotherham: The length of each curtain twenty-eight cubits, and, the breadth four cubits, of each curtain, one measure, for all the curtains.
text.DRB: The length of one curtain shall be twenty-eight cubits, the breadth shall be four cubits. All the curtains shall be of one measure.

text.NRSV: Five curtains shall be joined to one another; and the other five curtains shall be joined to one another.
text.Rotherham: The five curtains, shall be joined, one to another, and, the other five curtains, joined, one to another.
text.DRB: Five curtains shall be joined one to another, and the other five shall be coupled together in like manner.

text.NRSV: You shall make loops of blue on the edge of the outermost curtain in the first set; and likewise you shall make loops on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second set.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make loops of blue on the border of each curtain, at the edge of the set,—and, thus, shalt thou do in the border of the curtain, that is outmost in the second set:
text.DRB: Thou shalt make loops of violet in the sides and tops of the curtains, that they may be joined one to another.

text.NRSV: You shall make fifty loops on the one curtain, and you shall make fifty loops on the edge of the curtain that is in the second set; the loops shall be opposite one another.
text.Rotherham: fifty loops, shalt thou make, in the one curtain, and fifty loops, shalt thou make in the edge of the curtain which is in the second set, the loops being opposite, one to another.
text.DRB: Every curtain shall have fifty loops on both sides, so set on, that one loop may be against another loop, and one may be fitted to the other.

text.NRSV: You shall make fifty clasps of gold, and join the curtains to one another with the clasps, so that the tabernacle may be one whole.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make fifty clasps of gold,—so shalt thou join the curtains one to another, with the clasps, and thus shall the habitation be one.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also fifty rings of gold wherewith the veils of the curtains are to be joined, that it may be made one tabernacle.

text.NRSV: You shall also make curtains of goats’ hair for a tent over the tabernacle; you shall make eleven curtains.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make curtains of goat’s hair, for a tent, over the habitation—eleven curtains, shalt thou make them.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also eleven curtains of goats' hair, to cover the top of the tabernacle.

text.NRSV: The length of each curtain shall be thirty cubits, and the width of each curtain four cubits; the eleven curtains shall be of the same size.
text.Rotherham: The length of each curtain, thirty cubits, and the breadth, four cubits, of each curtain,—one measure, to the eleven curtains.
text.DRB: The length of one hair curtain shall be thirty cubits: and the breadth four: the measure of all the curtains shall be equal.

text.NRSV: You shall join five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves, and the sixth curtain you shall double over at the front of the tent.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt join the five curtains by themselves, and the six curtains by themselves, and thou shalt fold back the sixth curtain, in the forefront of the tent.
text.DRB: Five of which thou shalt couple by themselves, and the six others thou shalt couple one to another, so as to double the sixth curtain in the front of the roof.

text.NRSV: You shall make fifty loops on the edge of the curtain that is outermost in one set, and fifty loops on the edge of the curtain that is outermost in the second set.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make fifty loops on the border of the one curtain, that is outmost in the set, and fifty loops on the border of the curtain, of the second set.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also fifty loops in the edge of one curtain, that it may be joined with the other: and fifty loops in the edge of the other curtain, that it may be coupled with its fellow.

text.NRSV: You shall make fifty clasps of bronze, and put the clasps into the loops, and join the tent together, so that it may be one whole.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt make fifty clasps of bronze, and bring the clasps into the loops, so shalt thou join the tent, and it shall be one.
text.DRB: Thou shalt make also fifty buckles of brass, wherewith the loops may be joined, that of all there may be made one covering.

text.NRSV: The part that remains of the curtains of the tent, the half curtain that remains, shall hang over the back of the tabernacle.
text.Rotherham: And the overplus that remaineth in the curtains of the tent,—the half curtain that remaineth, shall hang over at the back of the habitation.
text.DRB: And that which shall remain of the curtains, that are prepared for the roof, to wit, one curtain that is over and above, with the half thereof thou shalt cover the back parts of the tabernacle.

text.NRSV: The cubit on the one side, and the cubit on the other side, of what remains in the length of the curtains of the tent, shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle, on this side and that side, to cover it.
text.Rotherham: And, the cubit on the one side, and the cubit on the other side of that which remaineth, in the length of the curtains of the tent shall be hung over the sides of the habitation on this side and on that side to make a covering.
text.DRB: And there shall hang down a cubit on the one side, and another on the other side, which is over and above in the length of the curtains, fencing both sides of the tabernacle.

text.NRSV: You shall make for the tent a covering of tanned rams’ skins and an outer covering of fine leather.
